name: Cusco
summary: Cusco is generally safe for tourists with proper precautions, though altitude sickness affects 40-50% of visitors. While violent crime is rare, petty theft and pickpocketing occur, especially in tourist areas like Plaza de Armas. The city benefits from strong security presence due to its tourism importance.
quickVerdict: Cusco is one of Peru's safest tourist destinations with relatively low crime rates compared to other Latin American cities. The main challenges are altitude adjustment at 11,150 feet elevation and typical tourist-focused petty crime. Most visitors enjoy trouble-free experiences when following basic safety precautions and allowing time for acclimatization.

neighborhoods.name: Historic Center/Plaza de Armas
neighborhoods.description: Heavily patrolled tourist area with strong police presence, numerous restaurants, shops, and tourist services. Generally very safe during day and reasonably safe at night.

neighborhoods.name: San Blas
neighborhoods.description: Charming artist quarter popular with tourists and backpackers. Safe during day with craft shops and galleries, though narrow streets can feel isolated at night.

neighborhoods.name: San Cristobal
neighborhoods.description: Residential neighborhood near Sacsayhuaman with beautiful views. Quieter area that's generally safe but requires more caution at night due to fewer people around.

neighborhoods.name: Wanchaq
neighborhoods.description: Local residential area with train station. Less touristy with basic services but requires more awareness of surroundings, especially for solo travelers.

neighborhoods.name: Airport Area
neighborhoods.description: Industrial area around Alejandro Velasco Astete Airport. Some reports of taxi scams and overcharging, stick to official airport services.

neighborhoods.name: Outskirts/Peripheral Areas
neighborhoods.description: Areas outside central tourist zones pose higher risks, especially at night. Poor lighting, fewer people, and higher crime potential. Avoid walking alone after dark.

scams.name: Fake Tour Operators
scams.description: Unlicensed operators offer cheap tours to Machu Picchu and other sites with poor service, no insurance, and surprise charges.
scams.howToAvoid: Book through certified agencies with proper licensing, check reviews, and avoid street vendors offering tours at unusually low prices.

scams.name: Photo Animal Scams
scams.description: Women in traditional dress with llamas and alpacas pressure tourists into photos then demand high payments or refuse initially agreed prices.
scams.howToAvoid: Agree on exact price beforehand, have exact change ready, politely decline if unsure, and check animal welfare before participating.

scams.name: Counterfeit Money
scams.description: Fake soles bills mixed with real currency, especially near markets and tourist areas.
scams.howToAvoid: Use ATMs inside banks, check bills for security features like watermarks, count money twice, and be familiar with genuine currency appearance.

scams.name: Taxi Overcharging
scams.description: Unofficial taxis and some drivers quote inflated prices or add unexpected charges, particularly targeting tourists.
scams.howToAvoid: Use official taxis with yellow/black colors and license plates, agree on fare before starting, use rideshare apps like Uber when available, or arrange transport through hotels.

soloFemale.overview: Cusco is considered one of Peru's safer destinations for solo female travelers, with thousands visiting successfully each year. The historic center and San Blas areas have strong tourist infrastructure and police presence. However, catcalling and unwanted attention can occur, and standard precautions are advised for nighttime activities.
tips: Stay in well-reviewed accommodations in tourist areas like Historic Center or San Blas
Dress conservatively, especially in rural areas and religious sites - Peru has traditional values
Avoid walking alone at night, use official taxis or rideshare apps instead of street hails
Join group tours and hostel activities to meet other travelers and enhance safety
Trust your instincts and remove yourself from uncomfortable situations immediately

nightSafety.overview: Cusco is relatively safe at night in well-lit tourist areas with good foot traffic, but caution increases in quieter neighborhoods and outskirts. The historic center maintains reasonable activity until late evening, though solo walking is not recommended after 10 PM.
tips: Stick to main tourist areas like Plaza de Armas and well-lit streets with other people
Travel in groups when possible, especially when bar hopping or returning from restaurants
Use official taxis or pre-arranged transportation rather than walking long distances
Avoid displaying valuables, expensive electronics, or large amounts of cash
Stay sober enough to maintain awareness of surroundings and make good decisions

transport.metro: Cusco does not have a metro system. Public buses (combis) cost about 0.80 soles but can be crowded with pickpocket risks on busy routes.
transport.rideshare: Uber operates in Cusco but with limited availability. Cabify is also available. Both offer safer alternatives to street taxis with driver tracking and preset fares.
transport.taxis: Official taxis display yellow/black colors and license plates. Agree on fare beforehand as meters aren't used. Fares typically 5-10 soles within city center. Avoid unofficial 'pirate' taxis.
transport.tips: For airport transfers, use official services or pre-arranged hotel pickups. Walking is safe in tourist areas during day. Colectivos (shared vans) serve Sacred Valley destinations affordably.

customs: Greet people with handshake or cheek kiss (women), always say 'buenos dias/tardes/noches' when entering establishments
Punctuality is relaxed for social events ('Peruvian time') but be on time for tours and official appointments
Remove hats and sunglasses when entering churches, dress modestly in religious and traditional areas
Keep hands visible on table when dining, don't put elbows on table, and leave small amount of food to show satisfaction
Ask permission before photographing people, especially in traditional dress - tipping 1-2 soles is customary

health.overview: Altitude sickness is the primary health concern, affecting 40-50% of visitors arriving from sea level at Cusco's 11,150-foot elevation. Symptoms include headache, nausea, fatigue, and dizziness typically appearing within 6-24 hours.
health.water: Tap water is not safe to drink. Use bottled water for drinking and brushing teeth. Well-stocked pharmacies and restaurants serve bottled water readily.
health.vaccinations: No special vaccinations required for Cusco. Standard travel vaccines recommended. Consult doctor about altitude sickness medication like acetazolamide if you have history of problems.
health.altitude: Allow 2-3 days for acclimatization. Drink coca tea, stay hydrated, avoid alcohol initially, and rest upon arrival. Medical facilities available for severe altitude sickness cases.

emergency.usEmbassy: US Embassy Lima: +51-1-618-2000. Located at Avenida La Encalada cdra. 17 s/n Surco, Lima. For emergencies: +1-202-501-4444 from abroad, +1-888-407-4747 from US/Canada.

faq.a: Yes, Cusco is generally safe for tourists and ranks among Peru's safest cities. Violent crime against tourists is rare, though petty theft and pickpocketing occur in crowded areas. The city has strong police presence due to tourism importance.

Cusco is reasonably safe at night in well-lit tourist areas like Plaza de Armas, but caution is advised. Avoid walking alone after 10 PM, use official taxis, and stick to areas with good foot traffic and lighting.

faq.q: Is Cusco safe for solo female travelers?
faq.a: Yes, thousands of women successfully travel solo to Cusco each year. Stay in tourist areas, dress conservatively, avoid walking alone at night, and join group activities. Some catcalling may occur but serious incidents are uncommon.

faq.q: What areas should I avoid in Cusco?
faq.a: Avoid peripheral areas and outskirts of the city, especially at night. Be extra cautious in markets like San Pedro, avoid poorly lit streets, and stay away from areas with few people around. The airport area has some taxi scam risks.

faq.q: Is it safe to use public transport in Cusco?
faq.a: Public buses are generally safe but crowded with pickpocket risks. Official taxis with yellow/black colors are safer than street taxis. Uber operates with limited availability. Always agree on fares beforehand and avoid unofficial 'pirate' taxis.
